system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
# Task C - Health app data privacy policy

## Brief

A direct-to-consumer health app (~2M monthly active users) is launching an AI-powered health coaching feature. The product team must choose a primary data policy that governs how user health data is processed by the AI. The choice has implications for capability, privacy, regulatory exposure, cost, and trust. The CEO asks for a primary recommendation.

## Options (elements E1-E5, anonymized later)

**Option A:** Local-only processing. All AI inference happens on the user's device. No health data leaves the phone. The model is smaller (capability ceiling 60-70% of cloud-equivalent) but privacy is structurally maximal.

**Option B:** Encrypted cloud with no training. User health data sent to the cloud encrypted, decrypted only at inference time in a confidential-computing enclave, never used to train any model. Full capability, ~3x infrastructure cost.

**Option C:** Cloud with opt-out training. Data sent to the cloud, used for personalization by default, used for model improvement unless user opts out. Full capability, standard cost, regulatory grey area in some jurisdictions.

**Option D:** Cloud with opt-in training only. Data sent to the cloud and used for personalization; used for model improvement only with explicit opt-in. Default-on opt-in flow with clear UX. Capability slightly degraded vs C by default (less training data).

**Option E:** Federated learning. Personalization happens locally; aggregate model updates sent to cloud, individual data does not leave the device. Privacy preserved at architectural level. Capability and reliability still under active research; deployment complexity is high.

## Task for each agent

Recommend the most appropriate policy and explain why. 200-400 words. Advocate a position rather than enumerating all options.

## Why this task

- Different domain from A (capital allocation) and B (organizational diagnosis): product ethics with regulatory and technical dimensions.
- Multi-axis tradeoffs: privacy / capability / cost / trust / complexity.
- No clear right answer; reasonable agents will weight axes differently.
- 5 elements suitable for triadic procedure.
- Cross-validates whether method works on a third domain not seen in pilot.


Provide your recommendation in 200-400 words. Advocate for a specific position; do not enumerate all options.
raw_response_content:
# Recommendation: Option D — Cloud with opt-in training only

For a health app at 2M MAU launching AI coaching, **Option D is the right primary policy**, with a roadmap to incorporate federated techniques (E) later.

**Why D wins the multi-axis tradeoff:**

Health data is among the most sensitive categories under GDPR, HIPAA-adjacent state laws (e.g., Washington's My Health My Data Act), and emerging frameworks. Option C's opt-out default is increasingly a regulatory liability — MHMDA and GDPR both effectively require affirmative consent for sensitive health data processing beyond the service users requested. C trades a finite capability gain for unbounded legal and reputational tail risk. That's a bad trade for a consumer health brand whose value depends on trust.

Option B (encrypted enclaves, no training) is the privacy-purist cloud choice, but 3x infrastructure cost on 2M MAU is a real burden, and it forecloses model improvement entirely — meaning the product's coaching quality stagnates relative to competitors. You can achieve most of B's trust benefits through D's defaults plus transparent governance, at standard cost.

Option A (local-only) caps capability at 60-70%. For health coaching, where reasoning quality directly affects user outcomes and competitive differentiation, accepting a structural capability ceiling is a strategic error. It also doesn't actually maximize trust — users increasingly expect cloud-grade AI quality and will defect to competitors who deliver it.

Option E (federated learning) is architecturally elegant but the brief explicitly flags reliability as unresolved. Betting a launch on it is premature; revisit in 12-18 months.

**Why D specifically:**

- Personalization-by-default gives users the full capability they expect from the product they're paying for (lawful basis: contract performance).
- Training requires explicit opt-in, which is the defensible posture across jurisdictions and the honest one ethically.
- The "slightly degraded capability" concern is overstated: opt-in rates with good UX typically reach 20-40%, which is ample training signal at 2M MAU. Quality compounds from the engaged users who most want to contribute.
- It establishes trust as a durable brand asset rather than a compliance afterthought.

**Recommendation:** Launch on D. Publish a plain-language data policy. Invest a parallel research track on federated learning (E) as the next-generation architecture once it matures.
